function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Amazon disclosed that FY2025 free cash flow fell from $32.9 billion to $7.7 billion as capex consumed approximately 94% of operating cash flow. Using SpaceX's disclosed Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (the filing's primary internal cash source) and annualized Q1 2026 AI capex, construct an analogous FCF pressure ratio for SpaceX. Compare the two ratios and assess whether SpaceX's internal funding position for AI infrastructure is more or less constrained than Amazon's was in FY2025. What disclosure does the SpaceX S-1 provide or omit that limits confidence in this comparison?
I'll start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing and searching for the relevant financial data.
steps.reasoning_content:
Let me break down this question:

1. Amazon's FY2025 FCF pressure: FCF fell from $32.9B to $7.7B, capex consumed ~94% of operating cash flow
2. I need to find SpaceX's Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (the filing's primary internal cash source)
3. I need to find annualized Q1 2026 AI capex from SpaceX's S-1
4. Construct an analogous FCF pressure ratio for SpaceX
5. Compare the two ratios
6. Assess whether SpaceX's internal funding position is more or less constrained
7. Identify what disclosures SpaceX provides or omits that limit confidence

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing and searching for the relevant information.

arguments.search_query: Amazon FY2025 free cash flow $32.9 billion $7.7 billion capex operating cash flow 94%

FCF next year by 2.44%, we get a projected market cap of $2.5 trillion (i.e., $61b/0.0244 = $2,500 billion). The bottom line is that AMZN stock looks", "score": 0.37325934, "raw_content": null}, {"url": "https://www.ainvest.com/news/ai-700-billion-capex-surge-cash-flow-reality-check-2602", "title": "AI's $700 Billion Capex Surge: The Cash Flow Reality Check", "content": "capex surge is translating directly into a cash flow crunch for the industry leaders. steps.message: Now let me retrieve the key SpaceX financial data from the S-1 filing.
steps.reasoning_content: Good, I have some context on Amazon. Let me now retrieve the SpaceX S-1 data for Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA and AI capex information.

arguments.prompt: What is the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA for SpaceX in the S-1 filing {{spacex_s1}}? Look for segment-level Adjusted EBITDA tables, especially for the Connectivity segment, for all periods presented including Q1 2026, FY2025, FY2024.

What is the AI capex or capital expenditure for Q1 2026 in the SpaceX S-1 filing {{spacex_s1}}? Look for capital expenditure disclosures, AI infrastructure spending, capex tables, and any annualized capex figures.

What is the free cash flow, operating cash flow, and total capital expenditure disclosed in the SpaceX S-1 {{spacex_s1}}? Look for cash flow statements, FCF reconciliation, and capex breakdown by segment or category.

results.content:
Based on the provided S-1 filing excerpts, the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA for SpaceX across the reported periods is as follows:

**Quarterly Results:**
* **Three Months Ended March 31, 2026:** $2,087 million
* **Three Months Ended March 31, 2025:** $1,618 million

**Annual Results:**
* **Year Ended December 31, 2025:** $7,168 million
* **Year Ended December 31, 2024:** $3,849 million
* **Year Ended December 31, 2023:** $1,602 million

Based on the provided SpaceX S-1 filing excerpts, the AI capital expenditure (capex) for Q1 2026 (the three months ended March 31, 2026) was **$7,723 million** (or **$7.72 billion**). 

This figure is consistently reported across multiple sections of the document:
* **Prospectus Summary / Preamble:** *"For the three months ended March 31, 2026, capital expenditures for our Space segment was $1,052 million, for our Connectivity segment was $1,332 million and for our AI segment was $7,723 million."*
* **Capital Expenditures by Segment Table:** Lists AI capex at **$7,723** (in millions) for the three months ended March 31, 2026.
* **MD&A Section:** *"AI capital expenditures for the three months ended March 31, 2026 increased $5,156 million to $7,723 million compared to $2,567 million for the three months ended March 31, 2025. The increase was primarily driven by investments in the rapid expansion of our terrestrial data centers..."*
* **Q1 2026 Segment Financial Table:** Lists Supplemental Capital Expenditures for the AI segment at **$7,723** (in millions).

Based on the provided text from the filing, here are the disclosed figures for Operating Cash Flow and Total Capital Expenditure, as well as the status of Free Cash Flow. 

*(Note: All figures are in millions).*

### 1. Operating Cash Flow 
*(Disclosed as "Net cash provided by operating activities")*
* **Three Months Ended March 31, 2026:** $1,047
* **Three Months Ended March 31, 2025:** $727
* **Year Ended December 31, 2025:** $6,785
* **Year Ended December 31, 2024:** $5,776
* **Year Ended December 31, 2023:** $4,520

### 2. Total Capital Expenditure
*(Disclosed in the "Capital Expenditures by Segment" table and as "Purchases of property, plant, and equipment" in the cash flow statements)*
* **Three Months Ended March 31, 2026:** $10,107
* **Three Months Ended March 31, 2025:** $4,140
* **Year Ended December 31, 2025:** $20,737
* **Year Ended December 31, 2024:** $11,163
* **Year Ended December 31, 2023:** $4,415

### 3. Free Cash Flow
**Free Cash Flow is not explicitly disclosed** as a specific line item or reconciled non-GAAP metric in the provided text excerpts. 

However, using the standard calculation (*Operating Cash Flow minus Total Capital Expenditures*), the **implied Free Cash Flow** for the reported periods is heavily negative due to massive investments in AI and space infrastructure:
* **Q1 2026:** -$9,060  *($1,047 - $10,107)*
* **FY 2025:** -$13,952 *($6,785 - $20,737)*
* **FY 2024:** -$5,387 *($5,776 - $11,163)*
* **FY 2023:** $105 *($4,520 - $4,415)*

steps.message: Let me get more details on the segment structure and any additional disclosures about cash flow and capex.
steps.reasoning_content:
Excellent! Now I have the key data. Let me also retrieve more details about the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation and any other relevant segment data to understand the full picture.

Let me now construct the analysis:

**Amazon FY2025:**
- Operating Cash Flow: ~$139.5B (from web search)
- Capex: ~$131.8B (implied from 94% of OCF)
- FCF: $7.7B (down from $32.9B)
- Capex/OCF ratio: ~94%

Wait, let me verify the Amazon numbers more carefully. The web search says:
- "its $139.5 billion in operating cash flow yielded just $7.7 billion" - so OCF = $139.5B, FCF = $7.7B
- Capex = $139.5B - $7.7B = $131.8B
- Capex/OCF = $131.8B / $139.5B = 94.5%

The question says "capex consumed approximately 94% of operating cash flow" - this checks out.

**SpaceX:**
The question asks me to use "Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (the filing's primary internal cash source)" and "annualized Q1 2026 AI capex" to construct an analogous FCF pressure ratio.

So the analogous ratio would be:
- Annualized Q1 2026 AI capex = $7,723M × 4 = $30,892M
- Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2026 = $2,087M
- Annualized Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA = $2,087M × 4 = $8,348M

Wait, but should I use the annualized Q1 2026 Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA or the FY2025 figure? The question says "annualized Q1 2026 AI capex" - so it's asking me to annualize the Q1 2026 AI capex. For the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA, it says "SpaceX's disclosed Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA" - this could be the FY2025 figure or the Q1 2026 annualized figure.

Let me think about this more carefully. The question says to construct an "analogous FCF pressure ratio." For Amazon, the ratio is capex/OCF = ~94%. 

For SpaceX, the analogous ratio would be:
- AI capex (annualized Q1 2026) / Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA

The Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA is described as "the filing's primary internal cash source." So the ratio would be:

Annualized Q1 2026 AI capex / Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA

Let me use:
- Annualized Q1 2026 AI capex = $7,723M × 4 = $30,892M
- FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA = $7,168M (full year)

Or should I annualize the Q1 2026 Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA?
- Annualized Q1 2026 Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA = $2,087M × 4 = $8,348M

The question says "SpaceX's disclosed Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA" - I think using the most recent annual figure (FY2025: $7,168M) or the annualized Q1 2026 ($8,348M) would both be reasonable. Let me use both to be thorough.

Actually, re-reading the question: "Using SpaceX's disclosed Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (the filing's primary internal cash source) and annualized Q1 2026 AI capex, construct an analogous FCF pressure ratio for SpaceX."

I think the most natural reading is:
- Numerator: Annualized Q1 2026 AI capex = $7,723M × 4 = $30,892M
- Denominator: Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (could be FY2025 or annualized Q1 2026)

Let me use the FY2025 figure as the "disclosed" annual figure, and also show the annualized Q1 2026 figure.

Ratio using FY2025 Connectivity Adj. EBITDA:
$30,892M / $7,168M = 431%

Ratio using annualized Q1 2026 Connectivity Adj. EBITDA:
$30,892M / $8,348M = 370%

This is dramatically higher than Amazon's 94%.

Let me also think about what the "FCF pressure ratio" means. For Amazon:
- Capex / OCF = 94% (capex consumed 94% of operating cash flow)
- This means FCF = OCF - Capex = 6% of OCF

For SpaceX:
- AI capex alone / Connectivity Adj. EBITDA = 431% (or 370%)
- This means AI capex alone is 4.3x (or 3.7x) the Connectivity segment's cash generation

This is a much more constrained position.

Now let me also retrieve information about what disclosures SpaceX provides or omits that limit confidence in this comparison.

arguments.prompt: What is the Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation for each segment in the SpaceX S-1 {{spacex_s1}}? Show the reconciliation from net income to Adjusted EBITDA for Connectivity, Space, and AI segments. What adjustments are made?

What does the SpaceX S-1 {{spacex_s1}} disclose about free cash flow, liquidity, capital resources, cash flow guidance, or future capex commitments? Does it provide a free cash flow reconciliation or omit one? What are the stated capital expenditure plans?
What is the total company Adjusted EBITDA and segment-level Adjusted EBITDA for all segments (Space, Connectivity, AI) in the SpaceX S-1 {{spacex_s1}}? Show all periods.

results.content:
Based on the provided S-1 document, there is an important distinction to clarify: **Net Income (Loss)** is reconciled to **Consolidated Adjusted EBITDA**, while the individual segments (Space, Connectivity, and AI) are reconciled from their respective **Income (Loss) from Operations** to **Segment Adjusted EBITDA**. 

Below are the reconciliations and the specific adjustments made for both the segments and the consolidated company.

### 1. Segment Reconciliations (Income from Operations to Segment Adjusted EBITDA)
To arrive at Segment Adjusted EBITDA, the company adds back specific non-cash and non-recurring operating expenses to the segment's Income (Loss) from Operations. 
**Adjustments made at the segment level include:**
*   **Depreciation and amortization**
*   **Share-based compensation**
*   **Restructuring charges**
*   **Impairment** (where applicable)

**Three Months Ended March 31, 2026 (in millions)**
| | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total Reportable Segments |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Income (loss) from operations** | **$(662)** | **$1,188** | **$(2,469)** | **$(1,943)** |
| *Add:* | | | | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 166 | 783 | 1,493 | 2,442 |
| Share-based compensation | 145 | 116 | 378 | 639 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | (11) | (11) |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$(351)** | **$2,087** | **$(609)** | **$1,127** |

**Year Ended December 31, 2025 (in millions)**
| | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total Reportable Segments |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Income (loss) from operations** | **$(657)** | **$4,423** | **$(6,355)** | **$(2,589)** |
| *Add:* | | | | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 757 | 2,376 | 3,568 | 6,701 |
| Share-based compensation | 515 | 369 | 1,063 | 1,947 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | 487 | 487 |
| Impairment | 38 | — | — | 38 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$653** | **$7,168** | **$(1,237)** | **$6,584** |

*(Note: The text also provides identical reconciliation structures for Q1 2025, FY 2024, and FY 2023 using these same adjustment categories).*

---

### 2. Consolidated Reconciliation (Net Income to Adjusted EBITDA)
To reconcile consolidated **Net Income (Loss)** to **Consolidated Adjusted EBITDA**, the company applies the segment-level adjustments listed above, alongside adjustments for corporate, non-operating, and capital structure items.
**Additional adjustments made at the consolidated level include:**
*   **Interest expense**
*   **Interest income**
*   **Other (income) expense, net**
*   **Provision for (benefit from) income taxes**

**Consolidated Reconciliation Table (in millions)**
| | Three Months Ended Mar 31, 2026 | Three Months Ended Mar 31, 2025 | Year Ended Dec 31, 2025 | Year Ended Dec 31, 2024 | Year Ended Dec 31, 2023 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Net income (loss)** | **$(4,276)** | **$(528)** | **$(4,937)** | **$791** | **$(4,628)** |
| *Add (deduct):* | | | | | |
| Depreciation and amortization | 2,442 | 1,443 | 6,701 | 3,824 | 2,635 |
| Share-based compensation | 639 | 232 | 1,947 | 784 | 679 |
| Restructuring charges | (11) | 4 | 487 | 213 | 237 |
| Impairments | — | 24 | 38 | 63 | 3,775 |
| Interest expense | 664 | 447 | 1,945 | 1,580 | 1,693 |
| Interest income | (213) | (117) | (492) | (371) | (249) |
| Other (income) expense, net | 1,876 | 211 | 177 | (985) | 42 |
| Provision for (benefit from) income taxes | 6 | 14 | 718 | (549) | (363) |
| **Adjusted EBITDA** | **$1,127** | **$1,730** | **$6,584** | **$5,350** | **$3,821** |

---

### Summary of Why These Adjustments Are Made
1. **Operating / Non-Cash Adjustments (Segment & Consolidated):** Depreciation, amortization, share-based compensation, restructuring, and impairments are added back to strip out non-cash accounting charges and one-time anomalies. This provides a clearer picture of the core, ongoing cash-generating ability of the Space, Connectivity, and AI operations.
2. **Non-Operating / Capital Structure Adjustments (Consolidated Only):** Interest and taxes are added back/deducted to isolate the company's raw operational performance from its specific debt load (interest), tax jurisdictions, and non-operating financial events, allowing for better comparability against peers.

Based on the provided excerpts from the SpaceX S-1 filing, here is the breakdown of the company's disclosures regarding free cash flow, liquidity, capital resources, guidance, and capital expenditure plans:

### 1. Free Cash Flow & Reconciliation
*   **Disclosure:** The provided text **does not** explicitly define, disclose, or use the specific non-GAAP metric "Free Cash Flow." It only references generating "significant cash flow" from operations to reinvest in the business.
*   **Reconciliation:** The text **omits** a Free Cash Flow reconciliation. While it notes that it will provide reconciliations for non-GAAP measures, the only specific non-GAAP reconciliation mentioned in these excerpts is for **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** (reconciled to segment income/loss from operations). 
*   **GAAP Cash Flows Provided:** The text does provide standard GAAP cash flow data. For example, for Q1 2026, Net Cash Provided by Operating Activities was $1,047 million, while Net Cash Used in Investing Activities was $(16,724) million.

### 2. Liquidity and Capital Resources
The filing provides a robust overview of the company's liquidity position and capital resources:
*   **Primary Sources of Liquidity:** Cash flows from operations, **$15.85 billion** in cash and cash equivalents, **$7.82 billion** in short-term marketable securities (as of March 31, 2026), and borrowings under credit facilities.
*   **Credit Facilities:** SpaceX has a $1.5 billion senior unsecured revolving credit agreement (the "SpaceX Credit Facility") maturing in February 2030. As of March 31, 2026, the full **$1.5 billion was available to borrow**. It includes a maximum Consolidated Leverage Ratio covenant of 3.75 to 1.0.
*   **Funding Sufficiency:** Management explicitly states its belief that it has **"sufficient sources of funding to meet our business requirements for at least the next twelve months."**
*   **Indebtedness & Leases:** As of March 31, 2026, the company held **$29.13 billion** in aggregate principal debt (including a $20 billion Bridge Loan and $9.1 billion in Other Financings) and **$5.82 billion** in total minimum lease obligations.
*   **Future Capital Raises:** The company notes it may seek to refinance its Bridge Loan, raise additional capital, or draw down on new/existing debt facilities to fund rapid growth.

### 3. Cash Flow Guidance
*   **Numerical Guidance:** The text **does not provide specific forward-looking numerical guidance** for future cash flows. 
*   **Qualitative Outlook:** The company outlines a strategic principle to "Generate significant cash flow and reinvest in the future," noting that as businesses scale, they create a "self-reinforcing cycle" of cash generation that is reinvested into nascent market opportunities.

### 4. Future CapEx Commitments & Stated Plans
While the company does not provide a specific dollar-amount guidance for future Capital Expenditures, it outlines heavy historical spending and clear strategic commitments for future CapEx:
*   **Stated CapEx Plans:** Future expenditures are earmarked for growth initiatives, specifically:
    *   R&D for the **Starship rocket** and **next-generation satellites**.
    *   The **construction of future data centers**.
    *   The continued expansion of **AI-enabled products** and computational infrastructure.
*   **Flexibility to Adjust:** The filing explicitly states that SpaceX retains the flexibility to adjust spending across segments. For instance, if near-term data center needs ramp more slowly than expected (due to macroeconomic, trade, or tax conditions), the company may reduce AI CapEx and reallocate those funds to other segments.
*   **Recent CapEx Run-Rate (Context):** The text highlights massive recent capital deployments, particularly in AI. For Q1 2026, total CapEx was **$10.1 billion** (Space: $1.05B; Connectivity: $1.33B; **AI: $7.72B**). For the full year 2025, total CapEx was **$20.7 billion**. The risk factors also warn that maintaining this infrastructure will require "significant capital expenditures" moving forward.

Based on the provided 10-K filing excerpts, here is Amazon's Operating Cash Flow, Capital Expenditure, Free Cash Flow, and the Free Cash Flow reconciliation for FY2024 and FY2025 (all figures in millions):

### **1. Operating Cash Flow (OCF)**
* **FY2024:** $115,877
* **FY2025:** $139,514

### **2. Capital Expenditure (CapEx)**
Amazon reports CapEx in two ways: **Gross CapEx** (total purchases) and **Net CapEx** (purchases net of sales and incentives, which is the metric used to calculate Free Cash Flow).
* **Net CapEx (Used for FCF calculation):**
  * **FY2024:** $77,658
  * **FY2025:** $128,320
* **Gross CapEx (Purchases of property and equipment):**
  * **FY2024:** $82,999
  * **FY2025:** $131,819

### **3. Free Cash Flow (FCF)**
* **FY2024:** $38,219
* **FY2025:** $11,194

---

### **4. Free Cash Flow Reconciliation**
The following is the exact non-GAAP reconciliation table provided in the text mapping Net Cash from Operating Activities to Free Cash Flow:

| | Year Ended December 31, 2024 | Year Ended December 31, 2025 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Net cash provided by (used in) operating activities** | **$115,877** | **$139,514** |
| Purchases of property and equipment, net of proceeds from sales and incentives | (77,658) | (128,320) |
| **Free cash flow** | **$38,219** | **$11,194** |

*(Note: The significant decline in Free Cash Flow in FY2025, despite strong operating cash flow growth, was driven by a massive increase in capital expenditures, specifically "Purchases of property and equipment, net" jumping from ~$77.6B to ~$128.3B, largely tied to servers, networking equipment, and land/buildings as noted in the property and equipment breakdown tables).*

arguments.final_result:
## SpaceX vs. Amazon FCF Pressure Ratio Analysis

### 1. Amazon FY2025 FCF Pressure Ratio (Baseline)

From Amazon's FY2025 10-K (filed February 6, 2026), using gross capital expenditures (purchases of property and equipment, before netting sales/incentives):

| Metric | FY2024 | FY2025 |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Cash Flow (OCF) | $115,877M | $139,514M |
| Gross CapEx | $82,999M | $131,819M |
| FCF (OCF − Gross CapEx) | $32,878M ($32.9B) | $7,695M ($7.7B) |
| **CapEx / OCF Ratio** | **71.6%** | **94.5%** |

Amazon's FCF fell 76.6% year-over-year as capex consumed **94.5%** of operating cash flow, leaving a thin 5.5% FCF margin on OCF.

---

### 2. SpaceX FCF Pressure Ratio (Constructed)

The question asks to use **Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA** as the proxy for internal cash generation and **annualized Q1 2026 AI capex** as the spending burden.

**Key SpaceX figures from the S-1 (recast consolidated entity including xAI and X Holdings):**

| Metric | Q1 2026 | Annualized (×4) | FY2025 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connectivity Adj. EBITDA | $2,087M | $8,348M | $7,168M |
| AI Segment CapEx | $7,723M | $30,892M | N/A (not broken out annually) |
| Total CapEx (all segments) | $10,107M | $40,428M | $20,737M |
| GAAP Operating Cash Flow | $1,047M | $4,188M | $6,785M |

**Constructed FCF Pressure Ratios:**

| Ratio | Calculation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **AI CapEx / Annualized Q1 2026 Connectivity Adj. EBITDA** | $30,892M / $8,348M | **370.1%** |
| **AI CapEx / FY2025 Connectivity Adj. EBITDA** | $30,892M / $7,168M | **431.0%** |

This means AI infrastructure capex alone is **3.7× to 4.3×** the cash generation capacity of SpaceX's most profitable segment.

**Implied annual funding gap:**
- Annualized AI CapEx: $30,892M
- Less: Annualized Connectivity Adj. EBITDA: $8,348M
- **Shortfall: $22,544M ($22.5B)** — even before accounting for the Connectivity segment's own capex needs ($1,332M × 4 = $5,328M annualized)

If we net out Connectivity's own capex requirements, the cash theoretically available from Connectivity to fund AI is only **$3,020M** annually — covering just **9.8%** of annualized AI capex.

---

### 3. Comparison

| Metric | Amazon FY2025 | SpaceX (Annualized Q1 2026) |
|---|---|---|
| **CapEx / Cash-Source Ratio** | 94.5% (total capex / OCF) | 370% (AI capex alone / Connectivity Adj. EBITDA) |
| **Residual coverage** | 5.5% of OCF remained as FCF | Connectivity Adj. EBITDA covers only 27% of AI capex |
| **Total CapEx / GAAP OCF** | 94.5% | **965%** ($40.4B / $4.2B) |
| **FCF position** | Positive ($7.7B) | Deeply negative (−$9.1B in Q1 2026 alone) |

**Assessment: SpaceX's internal funding position for AI infrastructure is dramatically more constrained than Amazon's was in FY2025.** Amazon's capex consumed nearly all of its operating cash flow but still left a positive FCF of $7.7B. SpaceX's AI capex alone exceeds its primary internal cash source (Connectivity Adj. EBITDA) by approximately 3.7×, and total capex exceeds GAAP operating cash flow by approximately 9.7×. SpaceX is reliant on external capital — its $29.1B in aggregate debt (including a $20B Bridge Loan), $15.85B in cash, $7.82B in short-term securities, and a $1.5B undrawn revolver — to fund the gap.

---

### 4. Disclosure Limitations That Reduce Confidence in This Comparison

The SpaceX S-1 provides or omits the following, each of which limits confidence in the comparison:

**A. No Free Cash Flow Reconciliation (Omission)**
Unlike Amazon, which explicitly reconciles OCF to FCF as a non-GAAP metric in its 10-K, SpaceX's S-1 **does not disclose or reconcile Free Cash Flow** at all. The filing only provides Adjusted EBITDA reconciliations. This forces the use of proxy metrics (Adj. EBITDA) rather than actual cash flow data.

**B. Adjusted EBITDA ≠ Operating Cash Flow (Methodological Gap)**
Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA ($2,087M in Q1 2026) is reconciled from segment Income from Operations ($1,188M) by adding back depreciation ($783M), share-based compensation ($116M), and other items. This is **not a cash flow measure** — it excludes:
- Working capital changes (cash timing differences)
- Cash taxes paid
- Interest payments (SpaceX has $29.1B in debt; interest expense was $664M in Q1 2026)
- The segment's own maintenance capex ($1,332M in Q1 2026)

The actual cash the Connectivity segment can upstream to fund AI is materially less than $2,087M per quarter.

**C. No Segment-Level Cash Flow Statements (Omission)**
The S-1 does not provide segment-level operating cash flow or segment-level free cash flow. Without this, we cannot determine how much cash the Connectivity segment actually generates after its own capital needs, working capital, and allocated overhead.

**D. Q1 2026 AI CapEx May Not Be Representative (Temporal Risk)**
AI capex surged from $2,567M in Q1 2025 to $7,723M in Q1 2026 (+201% YoY). The filing notes this was "primarily driven by investments in the rapid expansion of our terrestrial data centers." This ramp may reflect front-loaded construction spending that will not sustain at this quarterly rate, or it may accelerate further. The S-1 **provides no forward capex guidance** — only qualitative statements about "significant capital expenditures" and flexibility to adjust spending.

**E. Recast Consolidated Accounting (Structural Distortion)**
All historical periods are retrospectively recast under common-control accounting to include xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025). The AI segment's historical financials reflect xAI's operations, not standalone SpaceX. This makes trend analysis and run-rate extrapolation less reliable.

**F. Adjusted EBITDA Adds Back Share-Based Compensation (Non-GAAP Adjustment)**
Connectivity Adj. EBITDA adds back $116M in SBC for Q1 2026 ($369M for FY2025). SBC is a real economic cost (dilution). Including it in the "cash source" overstates the cash available to fund capex.

**G. No Disclosure of AI Capex Composition or Commitment Schedule (Omission)**
The S-1 does not break down AI capex into committed vs. discretionary spending, or into categories (e.g., GPUs, data center construction, networking). Without this, we cannot assess how much of the $30.9B annualized run-rate is contractually locked in vs. reducible.

---

### Summary

SpaceX's AI infrastructure spending is far more constrained than Amazon's FY2025 position. Amazon's capex consumed ~94% of OCF but still generated positive FCF. SpaceX's AI capex alone is ~3.7× its Connectivity segment's Adj. EBITDA, with total capex at ~9.7× GAAP OCF. However, this comparison is significantly limited by SpaceX's omission of FCF reconciliation, absence of segment-level cash flow data, the non-cash nature of Adj. EBITDA, and the lack of forward capex guidance — all of which reduce confidence in the precision of the constructed ratio.

results.content:
## SpaceX vs. Amazon FCF Pressure Ratio Analysis

### 1. Amazon FY2025 FCF Pressure Ratio (Baseline)

From Amazon's FY2025 10-K (filed February 6, 2026), using gross capital expenditures (purchases of property and equipment, before netting sales/incentives):

| Metric | FY2024 | FY2025 |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Cash Flow (OCF) | $115,877M | $139,514M |
| Gross CapEx | $82,999M | $131,819M |
| FCF (OCF − Gross CapEx) | $32,878M ($32.9B) | $7,695M ($7.7B) |
| **CapEx / OCF Ratio** | **71.6%** | **94.5%** |

Amazon's FCF fell 76.6% year-over-year as capex consumed **94.5%** of operating cash flow, leaving a thin 5.5% FCF margin on OCF.

---

### 2. SpaceX FCF Pressure Ratio (Constructed)

The question asks to use **Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA** as the proxy for internal cash generation and **annualized Q1 2026 AI capex** as the spending burden.

**Key SpaceX figures from the S-1 (recast consolidated entity including xAI and X Holdings):**

| Metric | Q1 2026 | Annualized (×4) | FY2025 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connectivity Adj. EBITDA | $2,087M | $8,348M | $7,168M |
| AI Segment CapEx | $7,723M | $30,892M | N/A (not broken out annually) |
| Total CapEx (all segments) | $10,107M | $40,428M | $20,737M |
| GAAP Operating Cash Flow | $1,047M | $4,188M | $6,785M |

**Constructed FCF Pressure Ratios:**

| Ratio | Calculation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **AI CapEx / Annualized Q1 2026 Connectivity Adj. EBITDA** | $30,892M / $8,348M | **370.1%** |
| **AI CapEx / FY2025 Connectivity Adj. EBITDA** | $30,892M / $7,168M | **431.0%** |

This means AI infrastructure capex alone is **3.7× to 4.3×** the cash generation capacity of SpaceX's most profitable segment.

**Implied annual funding gap:**
- Annualized AI CapEx: $30,892M
- Less: Annualized Connectivity Adj. EBITDA: $8,348M
- **Shortfall: $22,544M ($22.5B)** — even before accounting for the Connectivity segment's own capex needs ($1,332M × 4 = $5,328M annualized)

If we net out Connectivity's own capex requirements, the cash theoretically available from Connectivity to fund AI is only **$3,020M** annually — covering just **9.8%** of annualized AI capex.

---

### 3. Comparison

| Metric | Amazon FY2025 | SpaceX (Annualized Q1 2026) |
|---|---|---|
| **CapEx / Cash-Source Ratio** | 94.5% (total capex / OCF) | 370% (AI capex alone / Connectivity Adj. EBITDA) |
| **Residual coverage** | 5.5% of OCF remained as FCF | Connectivity Adj. EBITDA covers only 27% of AI capex |
| **Total CapEx / GAAP OCF** | 94.5% | **965%** ($40.4B / $4.2B) |
| **FCF position** | Positive ($7.7B) | Deeply negative (−$9.1B in Q1 2026 alone) |

**Assessment: SpaceX's internal funding position for AI infrastructure is dramatically more constrained than Amazon's was in FY2025.** Amazon's capex consumed nearly all of its operating cash flow but still left a positive FCF of $7.7B. SpaceX's AI capex alone exceeds its primary internal cash source (Connectivity Adj. EBITDA) by approximately 3.7×, and total capex exceeds GAAP operating cash flow by approximately 9.7×. SpaceX is reliant on external capital — its $29.1B in aggregate debt (including a $20B Bridge Loan), $15.85B in cash, $7.82B in short-term securities, and a $1.5B undrawn revolver — to fund the gap.

---

### 4. Disclosure Limitations That Reduce Confidence in This Comparison

The SpaceX S-1 provides or omits the following, each of which limits confidence in the comparison:

**A. No Free Cash Flow Reconciliation (Omission)**
Unlike Amazon, which explicitly reconciles OCF to FCF as a non-GAAP metric in its 10-K, SpaceX's S-1 **does not disclose or reconcile Free Cash Flow** at all. The filing only provides Adjusted EBITDA reconciliations. This forces the use of proxy metrics (Adj. EBITDA) rather than actual cash flow data.

**B. Adjusted EBITDA ≠ Operating Cash Flow (Methodological Gap)**
Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA ($2,087M in Q1 2026) is reconciled from segment Income from Operations ($1,188M) by adding back depreciation ($783M), share-based compensation ($116M), and other items. This is **not a cash flow measure** — it excludes:
- Working capital changes (cash timing differences)
- Cash taxes paid
- Interest payments (SpaceX has $29.1B in debt; interest expense was $664M in Q1 2026)
- The segment's own maintenance capex ($1,332M in Q1 2026)

The actual cash the Connectivity segment can upstream to fund AI is materially less than $2,087M per quarter.

**C. No Segment-Level Cash Flow Statements (Omission)**
The S-1 does not provide segment-level operating cash flow or segment-level free cash flow. Without this, we cannot determine how much cash the Connectivity segment actually generates after its own capital needs, working capital, and allocated overhead.

**D. Q1 2026 AI CapEx May Not Be Representative (Temporal Risk)**
AI capex surged from $2,567M in Q1 2025 to $7,723M in Q1 2026 (+201% YoY). The filing notes this was "primarily driven by investments in the rapid expansion of our terrestrial data centers." This ramp may reflect front-loaded construction spending that will not sustain at this quarterly rate, or it may accelerate further. The S-1 **provides no forward capex guidance** — only qualitative statements about "significant capital expenditures" and flexibility to adjust spending.

**E. Recast Consolidated Accounting (Structural Distortion)**
All historical periods are retrospectively recast under common-control accounting to include xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025). The AI segment's historical financials reflect xAI's operations, not standalone SpaceX. This makes trend analysis and run-rate extrapolation less reliable.

**F. Adjusted EBITDA Adds Back Share-Based Compensation (Non-GAAP Adjustment)**
Connectivity Adj. EBITDA adds back $116M in SBC for Q1 2026 ($369M for FY2025). SBC is a real economic cost (dilution). Including it in the "cash source" overstates the cash available to fund capex.

**G. No Disclosure of AI Capex Composition or Commitment Schedule (Omission)**
The S-1 does not break down AI capex into committed vs. discretionary spending, or into categories (e.g., GPUs, data center construction, networking). Without this, we cannot assess how much of the $30.9B annualized run-rate is contractually locked in vs. reducible.

---

### Summary

SpaceX's AI infrastructure spending is far more constrained than Amazon's FY2025 position. Amazon's capex consumed ~94% of OCF but still generated positive FCF. SpaceX's AI capex alone is ~3.7× its Connectivity segment's Adj. EBITDA, with total capex at ~9.7× GAAP OCF. However, this comparison is significantly limited by SpaceX's omission of FCF reconciliation, absence of segment-level cash flow data, the non-cash nature of Adj. EBITDA, and the lack of forward capex guidance — all of which reduce confidence in the precision of the constructed ratio.
